coredata.dc:title: Fast sorting of retired lithium-ion batteries: An EIS and VAE-FINCH based soft clustering method
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Peng, Weiwen
Wu, Xinhong
Li, Ziyuan
Zhu, Rong
Tan, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: The rapid growth of electric vehicles has led to an increasing number of retired lithium-ion batteries, highlighting the need for efficient sorting methods for second-life applications. Traditional methods depend on time-consuming charge–discharge cycling procedures, limiting their practical application in large-scale battery processing facilities. To address this challenge, we propose a fast-sorting framework that integrates electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with an ensemble variational autoencoder (VAE)-clustering approach. First, we employ EIS to rapidly and comprehensively extract battery health-related data. Second, we develop an integrated architecture combining a VAE with a first integer neighbor clustering hierarchy (FINCH) algorithm, where the VAE incorporates reconstruction loss with specialized loss functions for state of health and direct current internal resistance. Third, we implement an ensemble-based soft clustering strategy that calculates probability distributions for robust sorting results. Experimental validation on a self-tested 381 retired batteries shows that the proposed method outperforms traditional sorting techniques in clustering accuracy and is approximately 8 times faster than charge and discharge-based methods.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Retired battery
Echelon utilization
Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy
Sorting

Comparison results of different clustering algorithms.

head.ce:title: Fast sorting of retired lithium-ion batteries: An EIS and VAE-FINCH based soft clustering method

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, No. 66, Gongchang Road, Guangming District, Shenzhen, Guangdong, 518107, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Science and Intelligent Emergency Technology, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Science and Intelligent Emergency Technology, Shenzhen 518107, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Science and Intelligent Emergency Technology, Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory of Fire Science and Intelligent Emergency Technology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Systems Engineering, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: organization=Department of Systems Engineering, City University of Hong Kong,Hong Kong, 999077,China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The rapid growth of electric vehicles has led to an increasing number of retired lithium-ion batteries, highlighting the need for efficient sorting methods for second-life applications. Traditional methods depend on time-consuming charge–discharge cycling procedures, limiting their practical application in large-scale battery processing facilities. To address this challenge, we propose a fast-sorting framework that integrates electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) with an ensemble variational autoencoder (VAE)-clustering approach. First, we employ EIS to rapidly and comprehensively extract battery health-related data. Second, we develop an integrated architecture combining a VAE with a first integer neighbor clustering hierarchy (FINCH) algorithm, where the VAE incorporates reconstruction loss with specialized loss functions for state of health and direct current internal resistance. Third, we implement an ensemble-based soft clustering strategy that calculates probability distributions for robust sorting results. Experimental validation on a self-tested 381 retired batteries shows that the proposed method outperforms traditional sorting techniques in clustering accuracy and is approximately 8 times faster than charge and discharge-based methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the global shift towards electric transportation accelerates, lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) have become the primary power source for electric vehicles(EVs) [1]. The LIBs is not only the most critical power component in EVs but also the most expensive, representing approximately half of the vehicle’s total cost [2]. Echelon utilization, which utilizes remaining value of retired LIBs, offers considerable economic and environmental benefits [3]. With the number of retired lithium-ion batteries from electric vehicles rises, echelon utilization are essential to facilitate their secondary use [4,5]. Before implementing echelon utilization, it is necessary to sort and regroup retired LIBs to reduce inconsistency among individual batteries. During long-term use, the discrepancies in capacity, internal resistance, and other key performance indicators among the batteries within a battery pack tend to diverge significantly. If these retired cells with poor consistency are directly combined for echelon utilization, the overall performance of this resulting battery pack would be adversely impacted by LIBs with low capacity and large internal resistance [6]. Accordingly, fast sorting and reasonable regrouping of retired LIBs are crucial for ensuring the safety of echelon utilization.

The sorting methods for retired LIBs can be classified into three categories based on the sorting indicators used in these methods: (1) static parameters-based methods, (2) dynamic parameters-based methods, and (3) multiple parameters-based methods [7,8]. Static parameters refer to the battery’s state characteristics when not in operation, which include capacity, internal resistance, open circuit voltage (OCV), and state of charge (SOC). Lai et al. [9] proposed a neural network-based rapid screening and reorganization method for large-scale retired LIBs, using capacity and internal resistance as sorting criteria. Liu et al. [10] used capacity, internal resistance, and OCV as indicators, introducing an improved Gaussian mixture model-based clustering method for sorting retired LIBs after appearance detection. However, the complex conditions of retired LIBs in practical applications limit this method’s ability to reflect their characteristics. Unlike sorting based on static parameters, dynamic parameter-based sorting relies on the parameters of LIBs during operation, making it more representative of their typical working conditions. These features include voltage curves, incremental capacity (IC) curves, and EIS, etc. Zhou et al. [11] used the IC curve under high charging rate to extract capacity and internal resistance features of LIBs, sorting LIBs with support vector clustering (SVC). Chen et al. [12] extracted features from charge and discharge voltage curves by the approximate low-rank representation to identify inconsistent battery packs with abnormal voltages. Liu et al. [13] proposed a rapid sorting method for retired LIBs based on multi-feature extraction from partial charging segment. Extracting multiple features from the same partial charging interval and employing self-organizing map algorithm as the sorting algorithm. Multiple parameters-based methods combine both static and dynamic indicators. Zhou et al. [14] proposed a sorting method that employs the ordering points to identify the clustering structure algorithm, generating a three-dimensional vector to represent the health features. This health vector comprises capacity data derived from a partial charging curve, as well as direct current resistance and polarization resistance obtained from pulse discharge tests. Jiang et al. [15] proposed a fuzzy clustering-based sorting method considering capacity, internal resistance, and the IC curve peaks as indicators. This method selects suitable clustering features and algorithms for each stage, improving amplification and enhancing the safety of the sorting. From the above literature, capacity and internal resistance are the most commonly utilized features for retired LIBs sorting, attracting extensive attention [16].

The state of health (SOH) is commonly assessed through capacity and internal resistance [17], Internal resistance refers to the resistance encountered by the current flowing within the battery during operation. It comprises two components: ohmic internal resistance and polarization internal resistance, both of which contribute to the direct current internal resistance (DCIR) [18]. Most sorting methods generally use the capacity calibration test to measure capacity and the hybrid pulse power characterization (HPPC) test to assess DCIR [19–21]. However, these testing procedures are often energy-intensive and time-consuming. For example, a capacity calibration test takes 7 to 8 h, while a HPPC test takes 4 to 5 h. Significant time and resource demands severely impact productivity and increase regrouping costs, which deviates from the efficiency objective of echelon utilization. Therefore, how to achieve fast sorting and regrouping is an important task and a key issue in the echelon utilization. To reduce testing time and improve sorting efficiency, an electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) technique is used to extract features. EIS is a powerful and non-destructive technique for analyzing electrochemical systems that provides a large amount of information about the internal properties of the batteries in a relatively short measurement time while maintaining the integrity of the battery [22]. Several studies confirm the feasibility of using EIS data for sorting retired LIBs. However, most current sorting methods still require EIS characteristics to be supplemented with additional data, such as battery capacity or pulse characteristic curves, resulting in time-consuming testing protocols. For example, Liao et al. [23] combined EIS-based indicators (i.e., charge transfer resistance) with capacity and pulse discharge voltage measurements, while Wang et al. [24] extracted features from both EIS data and short-time dynamic voltages to characterize the consistency of retired LIBs. In contrast, our proposed method requires only a single full charge to 100% SOC to standardize the initial state of retired batteries, followed by EIS measurement, thereby eliminating the need for multiple independent testing procedures and reducing overall testing complexity.

The literature review above demonstrates that sorting accuracy has been ensured, but three issues need further investigation: (1) time-consuming testing, (2) inflexible regrouping, and (3) the need for manual hyper-parameters setting in existing clustering methods. To address the time-consuming testing issue, this paper extracted features only based on EIS data. Capacity and DCIR data are only used for training and evaluation, shortening the entire time of capacity test and HPPC test. Regarding the issue of inflexible regrouping, previous sorting methods are almost based on hard clustering, which means that each battery is assigned to a single group. However, fixed assignment overlooks potential adjustments for retired LIBs at the boundary of clusters, which could be reassigned on the basis of practical considerations, such as meeting specific performance targets or achieving the desired number of batteries within a group for module assembly. In recent years, soft clustering and integrated clustering methods have emerged as a promising alternative. For example, Yin et al. [25] proposed a two-stage sorting method combining static and dynamic characteristics (discharge capacity, temperature rise, voltage curves), utilizing DBSCAN for preliminary sorting and K-means++ for final classification. Liu et al. [26] introduced an enhanced sorting method with feature selection and multiple clustering techniques, using a fusion mechanism to improve accuracy by combining K-means, fuzzy C-means, and other clustering algorithms. These soft clustering and integrated clustering methods allows for a more granular consideration of battery characteristics and application requirements, leading to potentially significant improvements in overall system performance and resource utilization [27]. For the issue of manual hyper-parameters setting, most existing clustering methods require subjective choices for hyper-parameters, such as number of clusters and distance thresholds, which can be unstable and require adjustment for each dataset. This subjectivity complicates clustering by restricting a standard solution across different problems. To overcome this limitation, this paper adopts the first integer neighbor clustering hierarchy (FINCH) algorithm [28] to achieve an efficient and completely parameter-free sorting. The FINCH algorithm identifies cluster links by observing the first neighbor of each data point. Because the method does not rely on predefined similarity thresholds or other specific objectives, it shows potential for discovering natural clusters in the data. To solve the aforementioned problems, a fast and flexible sorting method based on the SOH-DCIR constrained variational autoencoder (VAE)-FINCH model is proposed, leveraging EIS for feature extraction. First, EIS is used to quickly and comprehensively obtain battery health related features, which are then fed into the VAE for capturing deep hierarchical structures within these features. The VAE used in this paper is improved by introducing loss functions that directly related to the SOH and DCIR of retired LIBs. This innovative improvement enables VAE to automatically extract encoded health indicators highly related to the SOH and DCIR, greatly improving the effectiveness of the VAE in generating encoded health indicators. Following the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE’s encoding, the FINCH clustering algorithm is used to process the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-encoded health indicators, obtaining the corresponding sorting results. To further improve the flexibility of regrouping, an ensemble strategy is used to calculate the probability of each sorted cells, allowing for flexible and reasonable grouping. In particular, by training the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE model multiple times, diverse sets of encoded health indicators are obtained, which are then input into the FINCH clustering algorithm to obtain multiple clustering results. Finally, the ensemble strategy is used to calculate the probability of each sorted cell, achieving robust soft clustering of retired batteries.
The main contributions can be summarized as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: A SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH fast sorting method based on EIS data is proposed for retired LIBs. This method has been verified on self-tested dataset with 381 retired batteries, achieving reductions of up to 66.76% and 14.09% in the sums of SOH and DCIR standard deviations, while reducing the time by nearly 8 times.

SOH and DCIR constraints are applied to the intermediate VAE outputs, which enables VAE to automatically extract encoded health indicators that are highly correlated with SOH and DCIR. This adaptation makes encoded health indicators more suitable for FINCH-based sorting, resulting in more reasonable results.

An ensemble strategy is implemented to calculate the probability of each sorted cell, providing probability-based regrouping rather than fixed cluster regrouping. This flexible approach provides more detailed information, leading to more adaptable regrouping results.

ce:section.ce:para: The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. Section 2 introduces the tests of 381 retired LIBs. Section 3 introduces the proposed method with descriptions of the framework and critical techniques. Section 4 demonstrates the proposed method with implementation details, experimental results and method comparisons. Section 5 summarizes the main research findings.

The dataset consists of 381 retired batteries with a nominal capacity of 2.6 Ah. The retired batteries are commercial ICR18650 lithium-ion batteries with graphite-based anodes, retired from electric vehicle applications, where aging is mainly dominated by solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) growth, loss of active lithium inventory, and increased charge-transfer resistance. The experimental platform used is shown in Fig. 1. The battery charging and discharging test system (NEWARE BTS-5V12 A) is used for long-term battery charging and discharging cycles, capacity calibration and HPPC testing. The multi-channel electrochemical workstation (Solartron analytical 1470E) is utilized to obtain the EIS spectrum of the retired LIBs, recording data such as frequency, real impedance, imaginary impedance, etc. The NEWARE-BTS test software on the computer is utilized to program the charging and discharging tests, as well as to record and export the test data. The Solartron-Cell test software is used to set up the EIS test program, record, and export the test data. The constant temperature chamber (KOMEG KMT-150G) is used to maintain the environment temperatures required for all tests.

For EIS tests, the SOC was adjusted to 100%. The retired LIBs were charged using a constant current and constant voltage (CC-CV) method at 1 C (2 A) until reaching a cut-off voltage of 4.2 V, with a cut-off current of 0.02 C (0.04 A). After charging, the retired LIBs were placed in the EIS test fixture, and the thermostat temperature was set to
20
∘
C
. The Solartron-Cell test software was used to set the EIS measurement parameters and to start the test. Key parameter settings include excitation voltage amplitude of 10 mV, frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 5000 Hz, and a sampling frequency of 25 frequency points per order of magnitude. EIS testing was performed at SOC = 100% to ensure consistency, as the SOC of retired batteries is often unknown and uncertain. Charging the battery fully before testing provides a standardized condition, avoiding the need for additional capacity calibration test required to determine SOC levels.

To ensure high-quality EIS data, the testing environment must be strictly controlled, including factors such as temperature and humidity. In large-scale scenarios, automated EIS testing systems can be used to maintain consistency, enabling high-throughput testing. While all battery testing methods require controlled environments, EIS offers a significant advantage by reducing testing time and cost compared to traditional charge–discharge cycles. Although the initial setup for EIS requires a capital investment, its ability to conduct rapid testing at scale ultimately reduces overall costs.

The EIS Nyquist plots of all 381 retired LIBs used in this paper are displayed in Fig. 2, where the horizontal axis represents the real part of the impedance and the vertical axis represents the imaginary part.

In this paper, the SOH and DCIR data are only used for training and evaluation purposes. Specifically, SOH and DCIR are added to the loss function model training to extract encoded health indicators correlated to SOH and DCIR. Additionally, the standard deviation (SDs) of SOH and DCIR values of the sorting results are calculated to verify the proposed model’s effectiveness. Notably, once the proposed method has been trained, SOH and DCIR data are not required when sorting retired LIBs, which can greatly reduce the overall time required for capacity calibration test and HPPC test.

To measure DCIR, the HPPC tests were conducted on the retired LIBs at 25
∘
C
. The current and voltage curves of HPPC test are shown in Fig. 3. Initially, retired LIBs were charged at CC-CV of 1 C (2 A) to a cut-off voltage of 4.2 V and a cut-off current of 0.02 C (0.04 A). After a 60-minute rest, the LIBs were discharged at constant current of 0.5 C (1 A) until reaching 10% SOC, equivalent to 200 mAh. This discharge process was repeated 10 times, with a 20-minute rest between each repetition. If the battery voltage dropped to 2.7 V during the test, the test was terminated. Finally, according to the voltage change before and after the pulse, DCIR was automatically obtained by the test software using Eq. (1).
(1)
D C I R =
V
1
−
V
2
I
1
−
I
2
,
where
V
1
and
V
2
are the voltage readings before and after the pulse,
I
1
and
I
2
are the corresponding current values.

The capacity calibration test adopted a two-stage discharge method to obtain the maximum remaining capacity of retired LIBs, combined with CC-CV charging, ensuring fully charge and discharge cycles for the LIBs. The thermostat was set to 25
∘
C, and the current and voltage curves of the capacity calibration test are shown in Fig. 4. First, the retired LIBs were charged at 1 C (2 A) CC-CV to a cut-off voltage 4.2 V and a cut-off current 0.02 C (0.04 A). After a 30-minute rest, the first discharge was performed at a rate of 1C (2 A) until the voltage reached 2.75 V. Following a 10-minute rest, the second discharge was performed at 0.05C (0.1 A) until the voltage reached 2.75 V again. After a 5-minute rest, the LIBs were charged at 1 C (2 A) CC-CV to a cut-off voltage of 4.2 V and a cut-off current of 0.02 C (0.04 A) again. Finally, the maximum remaining discharge capacity was calculated as the sum of the two-stage discharge capacities, while the maximum remaining charge capacity was the CC-CV charge capacity in the last step.
In this section, the fast and flexible sorting method with the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH model based on EIS data is introduced for retired LIBs.

The proposed framework includes four main steps, as shown in Fig. 5: (1) feature extraction from EIS data, (2) feature encoding based on SOH-DCIR constrained VAE, (3) FINCH-based sorting, and (4) ensemble-based soft regrouping. The details of these four steps are as follows:

ce:list-item.ce:para: Step 1. Feature extraction from EIS data: The Nyquist plot of EIS data is first adopted to extract electrochemical features, which effectively reveal the electrochemical reaction kinetics and charge transfer process within the retired LIBs. Furthermore, an equivalent circuit model (ECM) based on the EIS data is used to characterize the electrochemical properties of the LIBs. This step focuses on capturing low-level electrochemical parameters, including internal resistance, charge transfer resistance, and diffusion resistance. These features provide an initial representation of their electrochemical state and degradation mechanisms.

Step 2. Health indicators generation based on SOH-DCIR constrained VAE: A SOH-DCIR constrained VAE model is then constructed, and the extracted features from Step 1 are fed into the VAE model to generate encoded health indicators. The VAE is trained to automatically extract latent representations of battery health that are highly correlated with the SOH and DCIR. To further enhance the VAE model, the estimated errors of SOH and DCIR are incorporated into the loss function of the intermediate VAE outputs, ensuring that the encoded health indicators are optimized to reflect both the SOH and DCIR. It is worth noting that once the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE is trained, SOH and DCIR are no longer required for sorting the LIBs, as the encoded health indicators generated by the VAE can be directly used for the sorting tasks.

Step 3. FINCH-based sorting: After SOH-DCIR constrained VAE completes health indicators generating, the FINCH clustering algorithm is used to realize efficiently sort the retired LIBs, using the VAE-encoded health indicators as model input.

Step 4. Ensemble-based soft regrouping: To achieve soft clustering, Steps 2 and 3 are repeated multiple times. This process includes training the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE model multiple times to generate different encoded health indicators, which are then fed into the FINCH clustering algorithm separately, obtaining multiple clustering results. An ensemble strategy is then applied to calculate the probability that each retired LIB belongs to different groups, making the regrouping more flexible and reasonable, which directly impacts the economic viability and safety of echelon utilization. For practical module assembly, each battery can be automatically assigned to a specific group by selecting the group with the maximum probability obtained from the ensemble results, enabling deterministic and reproducible final grouping.

ce:section.ce:para: Following the above procedure, the fast sorting and reasonable regrouping of retired LIBs can be achieved.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feature extraction from the EIS data
Features extracted from the nyquist plot of EIS data
ce:section.ce:para: The Nyquist plots of EIS data encompass comprehensive spectral information, effectively decoupling the charge transfer, interfacial reactions, and diffusion processes within the electrode [29]. Since SOH and DCIR are key indicators for sorting retired LIBs but not directly measured in the proposed method, features closely related to the battery health are then extracted to indirectly characterize these two indicators. The Nyquist plots of EIS data with different SOHs and DCIRs are shown in Fig. 6. Analyzing these plots reveals that as SOH decreases and DCIR increases, the ohmic resistance, the diameter of the semicircle in the MF section, and the real and imaginary impedances of the LF section all increases significantly. All Nyquist-plot-based features are defined according to fixed geometric rules under identical testing conditions. Features are manually extracted based on the geometric variation of graphics and their corresponding aging mechanisms, as shown in Fig. 7 and described as follows.

ce:list-item.ce:para:
F 1
: The real part of the intersection with the horizontal axis, which represents ohmic resistance of the LIBs. As shown in Fig. 6,
F 1
increases significantly as SOH decreases and DCIR increases.

F 2
,
F 3
: The imaginary part (
F 2
) and amplitude (
F 3
) of an inflection point between two arc segments. The amplitude of a point refers to the vector length on the entire complex impedance plane, calculated as:
(2)
A m p l i t u d e =
Z
r e
2
+
Z
i m
2
,
where
Z
r e
and
Z
i m
represents the real and imaginary part of impedance, respectively.
F 2
and
F 3
reflect the resistance associated with the aging of the solid electrolyte interface phase in LIBs.

F 4
: The imaginary part of the vertex of the second arc (
F 4
), which reflects surface reactions of active materials, diffusion of electrolytes, and cathodic polarization processes at electrodes of the LIBs.

F 5
: The difference between the imaginary parts of the low-frequency inflection point and the last point on the impedance curve (
F 5
). This feature reflects the variation of impedance in the LF part.

ce:section.ce:para:
As the usage time of LIBs increases, surface reaction of active material may slow down, the diffusion of the electrolyte may be hindered, and the cathode polarization at the electrode may intensify, leading to changes in features
F 1
-
F 5
as described above. Therefore, SOH can be indirectly characterized by these five features. In addition, DCIR is mainly composed by ohmic resistance and polarization resistance, where ohmic resistance is represented by feature
F 1
. Polarization resistance is mainly related to electrochemical reactions, reflecting the difficulty of charge transfer and ion diffusion in the battery during current flow, these processes can be represented by features
F 2
-
F 5
. Accordingly, features
F 1
-
F 5
can be used to characterize the DCIR of retired LIBs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Features extracted from the ECM based on EIS data
ce:section.ce:para:
In addition to extracting features from the Nyquist plot of EIS data, the parameters of the ECM based on EIS data are obtained and used as features. The ECM simplifies the complex processes occurring within the LIBs by using a straightforward circuit model. Each circuit component in the ECM corresponds to a different battery internal side reaction, and the combination of these internal side reactions represents the battery’s aging behavior. In this paper, a second-order ECM is used, as illustrated in Fig. 8. The impedance at UHF part is represented by an ideal inductance L , and the ohmic impedance is represented by the resistance
R
o
. The impedance increase in the HF and MF parts is mainly result from lithium-ion inventory loss and the loss of positive electrode active materials. The ECM uses constant phase element (CPE) and
R
S E I
or
R
c t
elements in parallel to simulate the impedance changes in these two parts, respectively, describing the diffusion and surface reaction.
R
S E I
characterizes the impedance of the solid electrolyte interphase, while
R
c t
characterizes the charge transfer impedance. The
C P E
describes the phenomenon in which charges accumulate at the electrode interface when a current is applied [30]. The
C P E
is characterized by its capacitance and can be defined as follows:
(3)
C P E =
1
( j ω )
n
Q
.

Here, w is the angular frequency, j is the imaginary unit. n is the exponent of
C P E
, usually a decimal between 0 and 1, indicating the nonlinear characteristics of
C P E
. Q is the capacitance value of the
C P E
.

The impedance of the LF part is presented as a diagonal line corresponding to lithium-ions diffusion within the solid particles of the electrodes, which is generally described by the Warburg impedance
Z
W
[31,32]. As the internal electrode structure collapses due to the degradation, the diffusion of lithium ions diminishes.
Z
W
can be obtained as follows:
(4)
Z
W
=
σ
ω
( 1 − j )
,
where σ represents the Warburg coefficient, w is the angular frequency.

Using the second-order ECM depicted in Fig. 8 and the EIS data, the nonlinear least squares method is employed to identify the ECM parameters. The constant phase elements
C P
E
1
,
C P
E
2
and the warburg impedance
Z
W
are adopted to characterize the SOH and DCIR.

In summary, to comprehensively characterize the SOH and DCIR of retired LIBs, eight features extracted from the EIS data, leveraging the Nyquist plot and ECM, are selected:
F 1
,
F 2
,
F 3
,
F 4
,
F 5
,
C P
E
1
,
C P
E
2
, and
Z
W
. The features extracted in Step 1 (
F 1
,
F 2
,
F 3
,
F 4
,
F 5
,
C P
E
1
,
C P
E
2
, and
Z
W
) each capture different aspects of the battery’s impedance characteristics. The correlations between these features are limited, as shown in Fig. 9, and their individual correlations with SOH and DCIR are weak. However, in Step 2, the VAE is used to generate health indicators (SOH indicator and DCIR indicator), which exhibit strong correlations with SOH and DCIR, respectively.

To realize fast sorting, a combined VAE and FINCH method, called SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH, is proposed. To obtain a latent space more suitable for FINCH, the features extracted from EIS data are fed into SOH-DCIR constrained VAE, which automatically extracts latent features referred to as encoded health indicators. To further the correction of these encoded health indicators with SOH and DCIR of retired LIBs, the estimation errors of SOH and DCIR are used as the loss function of the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE’s intermediate outputs. The SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-encoded health indicators are further processed by the FINCH clustering algorithm to obtain sorting results of retired LIBs, followed by an ensemble strategy that calculates the probability of each sorted cell belonging to a specific cluster, resulting in robust soft clustering outcomes.
The VAE is an unsupervised representation learning method with Kullback–Leibler (KL) regularization in the latent space, primarily used for generating new samples that resemble a given dataset [33].

The encoder of the VAE maps the input features, X = [
F 1
,
F 2
,
F 3
,
F 4
,
F 5
,
C P
E
1
,
C P
E
2
,
Z
W
], into a latent representation. It should be noted that the VAE aim to shape this latent representation to follow a specific probability distribution, and a multivariate Gaussian distribution is used. Additionally, the mean values of the latent distribution z are used as the encoded health indicators, which serve as the input for the FINCH in sorting retired LIBs. The decoder decompresses the latent representation to reconstruct the original input [34].

The objective of training a VAE is to minimize the reconstruction error between the original features and the reconstructed ones, while ensuring the latent representation is distributed according to the specified probability distribution. This is achieved by optimizing a loss function that combines a mean squared error (MSE) reconstruction loss with a KL divergence regularization term to penalize deviations from the Gaussian distribution. The loss function L used to train SOH-DCIR constrained VAE consists of three parts, and the equation is as follows.
(5)
L =
L
1
+
L
2
+
L
3
(6)
L
1
= MSE
(
H
1
, SOH )
+ MSE
(
H
2
, DCIR )
=
1
n
Σ
i = 1
n
(
h
1 i
−
SOH
i
)
2
+
1
n
Σ
i = 1
n
(
h
2 i
−
DCIR
i
)
2
where n is the number of training samples,
H
1
and
H
2
are outputs of the linear4 layer of encoder, as shown in Fig. 10.
(7)
L
2
= MSE
(
H
3
, SOH )
+ MSE
(
H

, DCIR )
=

(

)

is the output of the linear10 layer of decoder, as shown in Fig. 10.

ce:section.ce:section-title: First integer neighbor clustering hierarchy algorithm
ce:section.ce:para: The core concept behind FINCH is that the nearest neighbor of each sample, determined by distance, is adequate for the clustering process. This algorithm connects each sample to its first neighbors.

FINCH develops a cluster graph that illustrates both direct and shared first-neighbor relationships among samples within the input space [28]. An adjacency matrix M is defined for each pair of samples
( i , j )
in the dataset as follows:
(9)
M
( i , j )
=
1 ,
if j =
κ
i
1
or
κ
j
1
= i or
κ
i
1
=
κ
j
1
0 ,
otherwise
,
where
κ
i
1
represents the first neighbor of sample i . The adjacency matrix M establishes connections between each sample i and its first neighbor
j =
κ
i
1
, ensures symmetry through
κ
j
1
= i
, and links samples that share a common first neighbor
κ
i
1
=
κ
j
1
. Clustering is achieved by detecting the connected components within the adjacency matrix.

An illustrative example is given in Fig. 11 to explain the mechanics of FINCH. Fig. 11(a) shows 6 samples and their first neighbors. With this information, a 6 × 6 adjacency matrix, shown in Fig. 11(b), can be formed according to the Eq. (9). The adjacency condition
j =
κ
i
1
connects each object to their first neighbors, as the orange arrows shown in Fig. 11(c). The condition
κ
i
1
=
κ
j
1
connects objects with the same first neighbor, as the green arrows shown in Fig. 11(c), which links object A to object D and objects C to object B. The condition
κ
j
1
= i
ensures symmetry and connects any missing links in the chains, as illustrated by the blue arrows in Fig. 11(c), where object B is linked to object C. This figure displays the directed graph corresponding to the adjacency matrix, showcasing three distinct clusters. Out of six objects, four are directly connected, while the symmetric neighbors create two separate clusters.

A total of 381 retired LIBs were used to validate the proposed method. This section outlines the implementation details and experimental results. Additionally, the comparison and discussion of the sorting results generated by different clustering algorithms and state-of-the-art methods are provided.

The SOH and DCIR test data of the 381 retired LIBs are divided into 50 used for training and 331 used for evaluation. It is worth noting that once the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE model has been trained, SOH and DCIR are no longer required for sorting. This implies that in the sorting process, only a small number of LIBs need to be tested for SOH and DCIR, greatly reducing the overall test time. Specifically, 50 retired LIBs are randomly chosen from the overall dataset to serve as the training set for the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE. Following training, the features X = [
F 1
,
F 2
,
F 3
,
F 4
,
F 5
,
C P
E
1
,
C P
E
2
,
Z
W
] of remaining 331 retired LIBs are fed into the trained SOH-DCIR constrained VAE encoder to obtain health indicators, represented by the mean values derived from the probability distribution produced by the encoder. These health indicators are then utilized as inputs for the FINCH clustering algorithm for sorting of these retired LIBs. In order to eliminate the dimensionality impact between features, the data is normalized by Z-Score firstly. To verify the representativeness of the training set, a statistical comparison of SOH and DCIR distributions was conducted between the training set, test set, and the overall dataset. As shown in Fig. 12, boxplots, density histograms, and empirical cumulative distribution functions indicate that the training set exhibits similar distribution characteristics to the test set and the full dataset for both SOH and DCIR, confirming that the selected training samples are representative. The use of a limited training set is intentional and reflects practical application scenarios, where only a small number of batteries can be labeled, while most retired batteries remain unlabeled. The proposed EIS-based method achieves an 8-fold speed improvement compared to traditional charge–discharge methods. A full charge–discharge cycle at a 1C charge–discharge rate typically requires at least 2 h, excluding resting time between cycles, while EIS testing can be completed in approximately 15 min per battery. The hyperparameter settings are shown in Table 1.

The objective of sorting is to reasonably regroup retired LIBs with similar characteristic for echelon utilization. This paper evaluates the sorting results by quantifying the consistency of their SOH and DCIR. The SDs is employed to assess the consistency of parameters [14,35,36], and a smaller SD indicates better consistency. Thus, the consistency of SOH and DCIR is measured by calculating the SD within each cluster and the sum of these SDs. The clustering results obtained from the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH without ensemble are shown in Table 2 and Fig. 13, where the horizontal axis represents the SOH and the vertical axis indicates the DCIR. The retired LIBs are divided into 3 groups, indicated by different colors and shapes. For the clustering result obtained with the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH without ensemble, each retired LIB is deterministically sorted into one specific group. The calculation of SD is given as:
(10)
S D =
1
N
∑
k = 1
N
(
x
k
−
x
̄
)
2
,
where N represents the number of retired LIBs in a group,
x
κ
represents the SOH or DCIR of the κ -th retired LIB, and
x
̄
represent the mean value of SOH or DCIR value for the group.

A limitation of this deterministic clustering is its insufficient flexibility for regrouping. For example, assigning a battery positioned at the boundary between two groups to a single group can be suboptimal; such boundary batteries could be reassigned based on practical considerations, such as the required number of batteries per group. Soft clustering permits samples to be assigned to multiple categories with associated probabilities, thereby increasing the flexibility of regrouping. Therefore, an ensemble strategy is used to flexibly cluster retired LIBs. The SOH-DCIR constrained VAE is trained multiple times to obtain different encoded health indicators, and the FINCH clustering algorithm is implemented multiple times, each time with a different set of encoded health indicators, producing multiple sets of sorting results. Based on the multiple groups of results, the probabilities are calculated for each retired LIB belongs to different clusters. Fig. 14 displays a heat map of the clustering probabilities, where the horizontal axis corresponds to the battery numbers, the vertical axis denotes the group labels, and the color intensity reflects the likelihood that a retired LIB belongs to a specific group. Batteries exhibiting similar probabilities across multiple groups can be identified as boundary cases and reassigned according to application-specific constraints, such as desired group size or performance targets, while a maximum-probability rule can still be applied as the default automated assignment strategy.

In the heat map, red indicates a probability close to 1, indicating that a retired LIB is highly likely to belong to that specific group. On the contrary, green means a possibility close to 0, indicating that the LIB is highly unlikely to belong to that group. Batteries with color between red and green can be flexibly assigned to either group, which means could be reassigned based on practical considerations, such as the desired number of batteries in one group. This approach can increase the flexibility and rationality of regrouping large-scale retired LIBs.

This section evaluates the performance of the proposed feature extraction method combined with a different clustering technique to show the superiority of the proposed VAE-FINCH clustering algorithm. The SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH are compared with FINCH, K-means, Gaussian mixture model (GMM), fuzzy c-means (FCM), SVC, Self-organizing maps (SOM) neural network, and Agglomerative clustering algorithm. The results are shown in Table 3 and Fig. 15. After applying SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH, a comparison of the SDs of SOH and DCIR within each LIBs group shows that the SDs are lower than the other clustering algorithms in most cases, with the smallest sum of SDs across all cases. The sums of SOH and DCIR SDs are reduced by up to 56.67% and 35.27% respectively. These grouping results indicate that, compared to other clustering algorithms, the SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH approach significantly reduces inconsistency within the sorted groups.

In this section, the feature extraction method in some state-of-art methods with their clustering technique to provide a direct comparison and demonstrate the advantages of the proposed framework. The following are the state-of-the-art methods used for comparison:

ce:list-item.ce:para: GMM-based method: Lai et al. [37] introduced a soft clustering approach utilizing GMM based on EIS. This method extracts multi-dimensional clustering criteria from EIS data, allowing for rapid capacity estimation using a neural network. Additionally, five other features are derived from the EIS data to facilitate the sorting of retired LIBs.

SVC-based method: Lee et al. [30] employed an electrical equivalent circuit model (EECM) in conjunction with particle swarm optimization (PSO) to extract seven features from EIS data for the classification of retired LIBs.
FCM-based method: Wang et al. [24] analyzed the consistency of retired LIBs using EIS and short-time dynamic voltage, applying the FCM algorithm for sorting.

ce:section.ce:para: The comparison results, presented in Table 4 and Fig. 16, reveal that the proposed sorting method yields the lowest sum of SDs. Specifically, the sums of SOH and DCIR SDs are reduced by up to 66.76% and 14.09%, respectively. These findings underscore the effectiveness of the extracted features and the proposed method, demonstrating its potential for the echelon utilization of retired LIBs.

While different battery materials and historical working conditions can lead to diverse clustering characteristics, the proposed VAE-FINCH clustering algorithm is flexible, combining the power of feature extraction through VAE with the adaptability of FINCH clustering. The proposed VAE-FINCH clustering algorithm simplifies integrated methods by combining feature extraction and clustering into a single, flexible approach that can adapt to various battery types and operational histories.

This paper proposed a method for fast sorting and flexible regrouping of retired LIBs using EIS data, reducing sorting time by nearly 8 times. The SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH method combined the strengths of both techniques, with the VAE enhancements improving the extraction of health indicators highly correlated with SOH and DCIR. The model was trained multiple times to generate different health indicators, and an ensemble strategy was used for probabilistic clustering, increasing regrouping flexibility. Compared with different clustering algorithms, the proposed method had reduced the sums of SOH and DCIR SDs by up to 56.67% and 35.27%, respectively, proving the effectiveness of SOH-DCIR constrained VAE-FINCH model. Furthermore, when compared to state-of-the-art sorting methods, the proposed method had achieved up to 66.76% and 14.09% reductions in the sums of SOH and DCIR SDs, respectively. The results highlight the effectiveness of the extracted features and the proposed method. This approach effectively reduced testing time and costs while maintaining high sorting accuracy, offering a practical solution for large-scale sorting of retired lithium-ion batteries.

Future work will investigate other battery systems by adapting impedance feature definitions according to their dominant aging mechanisms. Additionally, it will include long-term aging experiments with reassembled battery packs to assess the consistency of SOH and DCIR changes over time, while also exploring the integration of safety-related features, such as internal temperature rise and voltage fluctuations.

Weiwen Peng: Methodology, Conceptualization. Xinhong Wu: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Project administration, Investigation. Ziyuan Li: Writing – review & editing. Rong Zhu: Writing – review & editing, Methodology, Conceptualization. Xiaojun Tan: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare the following financial interests/personal relationships which may be considered as potential competing interests: Reports a relationship with that includes:. Has patent pending to. If there are other authors, they declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported in part by the National Natural Science Foundation of China with Project No. 72471251 and 725B2034, in part by the Shenzhen Science and Technology Program with Project No. JCYJ20250604175621029, and in part by the Shenzhen Sustainable Development Science and Technology Special Project (Double Car-bon Special Project, KCXST20221021111208019).

Kingma, D.P., Welling, M., et al., 2019. An introduction to variational autoencoders. Found. Trends Mach. Learn. 12, 307–392.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coupled VAE: Improved accuracy and robustness of a variational autoencoder

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Cao, S.C., Li, J.J., Nelson, K.P., Kon, M.A., 2022. Coupled vae: Improved accuracy and robustness of a variational autoencoder. Entropy 24, 423.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Regrouping and echelon utilization of retired lithium-ion batteries based on a novel support vector clustering approach

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, C.B., Wang, N.B., Li, W., Li, Y.S., Zhang, J.W., 2022. Regrouping and echelon utilization of retired lithium-ion batteries based on a novel support vector clustering approach. IEEE Trans. Transp. Electrification 8, 3648–3658.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: System-level performance degradation prediction for power converters based on SSA–elman NN and empirical knowledge

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zeng, Y., Huang, T., Zhang, T., Huang, H.Z., 2024. System-level performance degradation prediction for power converters based on ssa–elman nn and empirical knowledge. IEEE Trans. Ind. Informat. 20, 1240–1250.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Soft clustering of retired lithium-ion batteries for the secondary utilization using Gaussian mixture model based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lai, X., Deng, C., Tang, X.P., Gao, F.R., Han, X.B., Zheng, Y.J., 2022. Soft clustering of retired lithium-ion batteries for the secondary utilization using gaussian mixture model based on electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. J. Clean. Prod. 339, 130786.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Weiwen Peng received the B.E., M.E., and Ph.D. degrees in mechanical engineering from the University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, Chengdu, China, in 2009, 2012, and 2015, respectively. From 2016 to 2019, he worked as a Research Fellow with the National University of Singapore, Singapore. He is currently an Associate Professor with the School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University, China. His research interests include system reliability, Bayesian machine learning, prognostics, and health management.

Xinhong Wu received the B.E. degree in information management and information systems from the School of Management, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangzhou, China, in 2023. She is currently working toward the M.E. degree with the School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Shenzhen, China. Her research interests include battery health management and consistent sorting of lithium-ion batteries.

Ziyuan Li received the B.E. degree in data science and big data technology from the School of Statistics, Southwestern University of Finance and Economics, Chengdu, China, in 2024. She is currently working toward the M.E. degree with the School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Shenzhen, China. Her research interests include battery health management, machine learning, and Bayesian statistical methods.

Rong Zhu received the B.E. degree in automation from Xiamen University, Xiamen, China, in 2020, and the M.E. degree from the School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-Sen University, Shenzhen, China, in 2023. She is currently working toward the Ph.D. degree with the Department of Systems Engineering, City University of Hong Kong, Hong Kong. Her research interests include prognostics and health management, machine learning.

Xiaojun Tan received the Ph.D. degree from Sun Yat-sen University, Shenzhen, China, in 2005. From 2005 to 2010, he was a Lecturer with the Engineering School, Sun Yat-sen University, and was an Associate Professor from 2010 to 2017. Now, he is a Professor and also the Leader of the Electric Vehicle Research Center, School of Intelligent Systems Engineering, Sun Yat-sen University. His research interests include new energy vehicles, battery management systems, computer vision, and its application technology.
